signal: Vision-language-action models, simulation, teleoperation, and robot fleet data are converging into the software layer for useful physical AI.
whyItMatters: This is the layer that could make robots less scripted and more adaptable across real-world tasks.

signal: Factory, warehouse, and logistics pilots are turning humanoids from demos into measurable labor experiments.
whyItMatters: Labor deployment is where robotics shifts from spectacle to economic impact.

signal: Lower-cost humanoids, quadrupeds, actuators, sensors, and batteries are making robotics more accessible to labs and developers.
whyItMatters: Cheaper hardware expands the developer base and increases real-world experimentation.

signal: Fleet management, monitoring, safety systems, charging, simulation, and developer tools are becoming the hidden operating system for robotics.
whyItMatters: Robots scale only when the deployment layer becomes reliable, observable, and maintainable.

signal: Companion, eldercare, emotional AI, and home-assistive robots may enter households before fully general humanoids do.
whyItMatters: The fastest home adoption path may be emotional presence and care support, not full chore automation.

signal: Machine identity, DePIN networks, autonomous payments, and robot-owned economic activity are forming an early machine economy thesis.
whyItMatters: If machines become economic actors, robotics shifts from tools to autonomous network participants.
